Belgian association football club

This article is about the men's football team. For the women's team, see [Standard Liège (women)](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge_(women) "Standard Liège (women)").

**Royal Standard de Liège**, commonly referred to as **Standard Liège** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Standard de Liège* [[stɑ̃daʁ d(ə) ljɛʒ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Standard Luik* [[ˈstɑndɑrt ˈlœyk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Standard Lüttich* [[ˈstandaʁt ˈlʏtɪç, ˈʃtan-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) or simply **Standard** in Belgium, is a Belgian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").

They are one of the most successful clubs in Belgium, having won the [Belgian league](/wiki/Belgian_football_champions "Belgian football champions") on ten occasions, most recently in [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Belgian_First_Division "2007–08 Belgian First Division") and [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Belgian_First_Division "2008–09 Belgian First Division"). They have been in the top flight without interruption since 1921, longer than any other Belgian side. They have also won eight [Belgian Cups](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup"), and in [1981–82](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1981–82 European Cup Winners' Cup") they reached the final of the [European Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/1982_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_Final "1982 European Cup Winners' Cup Final"), which they lost 2–1 against [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Standard players are nicknamed *les Rouches* [[le ʁuʃ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") because of their red jerseys. The French word for red, *rouge*, when pronounced with a Liège accent, sounds like *rouche*.

In March 2022, Standard Liège was acquired by US-based private investment firm [777 Partners](/wiki/777_Partners "777 Partners").[[3]](#cite_note-3) In October 2024 it was announced that 777 Partners were declared bankrupt by a London court, making the future ownership unclear.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Standard_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

On the first day of school in September 1898, the pupils of [Collège Saint-Servais](/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_Saint-Servais_(Li%C3%A8ge) "Collège Saint-Servais (Liège)") in Liège started a football club, which they called Standard of Liège in reference to [Standard Athletic Club](/wiki/Standard_Athletic_Club "Standard Athletic Club") of [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) Standard, whose official name is Royal Standard Club of Liège, was based in Cointe and Grivegnée before settling permanently in 1909 in [Sclessin](/wiki/Sclessin "Sclessin"), an industrial neighbourhood in Liège.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) Standard initially joined the Belgian First League in 1909 before returning to the lower leagues a few years later. The club then gained promotion back to the top division in 1921 and has never been relegated since.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Standard_de_Li%C3%A8ge_%28ancien_logo%29.svg/250px-Standard_de_Li%C3%A8ge_%28ancien_logo%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Standard_de_Li%C3%A8ge_(ancien_logo).svg)

Club crest from 1923–1952

Shortly after World War II, [Roger Petit](/wiki/Roger_Petit "Roger Petit"), a former player and team captain, became general secretary of the club. Petit worked alongside President [Henrard Paul](/w/index.php?title=Henrard_Paul&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henrard Paul (page does not exist)") to establish Standard among the elite of Belgian football. In 1954, Standard won their first club trophy, the [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup"), which was soon followed by a first national title in [1957–58](/wiki/1957%E2%80%9358_Belgian_First_Division "1957–58 Belgian First Division").

At European level, in the 1960s, the club reached the semi-finals of the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") in [1961–62](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_European_Cup "1961–62 European Cup"), falling to beaten finalists [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") 0–6 on aggregate,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the same stage of the Cup Winners' Cup in the year [1966–67](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1966–67 European Cup Winners' Cup"), losing to eventual champions [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Standaard_Luik_tegen_Real_Madrid_beide_elftallen_en_Koning_Boudewijn_tijdens_wed%2C_Bestanddeelnr_913-7603.jpg/330px-Standaard_Luik_tegen_Real_Madrid_beide_elftallen_en_Koning_Boudewijn_tijdens_wed%2C_Bestanddeelnr_913-7603.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standaard_Luik_tegen_Real_Madrid_beide_elftallen_en_Koning_Boudewijn_tijdens_wed,_Bestanddeelnr_913-7603.jpg)

Standard faced [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") in the semi-finals of the [1961–62 European Cup](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_European_Cup "1961–62 European Cup").

The 1960s and early 1970s brought much success to the club, as Standard won six Belgian First Division titles, two Belgian Cups and a [League Cup](/wiki/Belgian_League_Cup "Belgian League Cup").

Driven by the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") [Ernst Happel](/wiki/Ernst_Happel "Ernst Happel"), Standard won the Belgian Cup again in 1981. The following year, [Raymond Goethals](/wiki/Raymond_Goethals "Raymond Goethals") took control of the team. Playing by the "Raymond Science" philosophy of football, the club was twice the champions of Belgium, twice winners of the Belgian Supercup (in three appearances) and reached the final of the European Cup Winners' Cup in [1982](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1981–82 European Cup Winners' Cup"). Standard played against [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in the [final](/wiki/1982_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_Final "1982 European Cup Winners' Cup Final") at the [Camp Nou](/wiki/Camp_Nou "Camp Nou") on 12 May 1982, losing the match 1–2 to the Spaniards.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1984, these exploits were tainted by the revelation of the [Standard-Waterschei Affair](/w/index.php?title=Standard-Waterschei_Affair&action=edit&redlink=1 "Standard-Waterschei Affair (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affaire_Standard-Waterschei "fr:Affaire Standard-Waterschei")]. Just days before the match against Barcelona, to secure the championship of Belgium and guard against last minute injuries, Standard had approached [Roland Janssen](/w/index.php?title=Roland_Janssen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roland Janssen (page does not exist)"), the captain of [Thor Waterschei](/wiki/K._Waterschei_S.V._Thor_Genk "K. Waterschei S.V. Thor Genk"), to ensure that Thor players threw the final game of the season.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) This scandal involved several players, including [Eric Gerets](/wiki/Eric_Gerets "Eric Gerets"), and coach [Raymond Goethals](/wiki/Raymond_Goethals "Raymond Goethals"), who fled to Portugal to escape suspension.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) In compensation the Standard players gave their game bonuses to the Waterschei players.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) Following the scandal, Standard was deprived of many of its playing staff due to long-term suspensions and it took the club several years to recover from the incident.

On 6 June 1993, Standard won the Belgian Cup for the fifth time in its history, defeating [Robert Waseige](/wiki/Robert_Waseige "Robert Waseige")'s [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi_SC "Charleroi SC") at the [Constant Vanden Stock Stadium](/wiki/Constant_Vanden_Stock_Stadium "Constant Vanden Stock Stadium") in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels").[[9]](#cite_note-Standard_History-9) This led to another appearance in the [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), ending in a record 10–0 aggregate defeat to [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")— having lost 3–0 at [Highbury](/wiki/Highbury "Highbury") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), Standard were humiliated 0–7 in the second leg at home.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Following the scandal of 1982, it took 25 years before Standard won the Belgium Championship again, lifting the title on 20 April 2008.[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) The club won the Belgian league again the following year, securing the club's tenth league title on 24 May 2009 after a home-and-away game against rivals [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht").[[4]](#cite_note-Rebel_Ultras_History-4) Standard won the national cup once more in [2011](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Belgian_Cup "2010–11 Belgian Cup"), defeating [Westerlo](/wiki/K.V.C._Westerlo "K.V.C. Westerlo") 2–0 in the [final](/wiki/2011_Belgian_Cup_Final "2011 Belgian Cup Final") at the [King Baudouin Stadium](/wiki/King_Baudouin_Stadium "King Baudouin Stadium") on 21 May 2011.[[9]](#cite_note-Standard_History-9) The club was bought by businessman [Roland Duchatelet](/wiki/Roland_Duchatelet "Roland Duchatelet") on 23 June 2011,[[11]](#cite_note-Belgian_Waffle-11) who then took over [English](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") club [Charlton](/wiki/Charlton_F.C. "Charlton F.C.") in December 2013, creating an affiliation between the two clubs.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Standard_liege_flag.jpg/250px-Standard_liege_flag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standard_liege_flag.jpg)

Flag waving at the [Stade Maurice Dufrasne](/wiki/Stade_Maurice_Dufrasne "Stade Maurice Dufrasne")

On 20 October 2014, [Guy Luzon](/wiki/Guy_Luzon "Guy Luzon") resigned as manager of Standard with the club sitting in 12th position in the [Pro League standings](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Belgian_Pro_League "2014–15 Belgian Pro League") and having taken only two points from three [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Europa_League "2014–15 UEFA Europa League") matches.[[13]](#cite_note-Luzon_resigns-13) Luzon later [became](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Belgian_First_Division "2005–06 Belgian First Division") head coach of Charlton.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Assistant and former midfielder [Ivan Vukomanović](/wiki/Ivan_Vukomanovi%C4%87 "Ivan Vukomanović") took over as caretaker-manager.[[13]](#cite_note-Luzon_resigns-13)

### Golden Shoe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Standard_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Golden Shoe")]

On nine occasions, Standard players have won the [Belgian Golden Shoe](/wiki/Belgian_Golden_Shoe "Belgian Golden Shoe") as the best player in the domestic league.[[15]](#cite_note-Standard_Domestic_Trophies-15) [Jean Nicolay](/wiki/Jean_Nicolay "Jean Nicolay") won the award in 1963, [Wilfried Van Moer](/wiki/Wilfried_Van_Moer "Wilfried Van Moer") in 1969 and 1970, [Christian Piot](/wiki/Christian_Piot "Christian Piot") in 1972, [Eric Gerets](/wiki/Eric_Gerets "Eric Gerets") in 1982, [Sérgio Conceição](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Concei%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Sérgio Conceição") in 2005, [Steven Defour](/wiki/Steven_Defour "Steven Defour") in 2007, [Axel Witsel](/wiki/Axel_Witsel "Axel Witsel") in 2008 and [Milan Jovanović](/wiki/Milan_Jovanovi%C4%87_(footballer,_born_1981) "Milan Jovanović (footballer, born 1981)") in 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-Standard_Domestic_Trophies-15)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Standard_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Standard_Liege02.jpg/330px-Standard_Liege02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standard_Liege02.jpg)

The stadium before a game in the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Belgian_First_Division "2005–06 Belgian First Division").

The stadium is popularly called Sclessin. That is not the official name, but [Stade Maurice Dufrasne](/wiki/Stade_Maurice_Dufrasne "Stade Maurice Dufrasne"). [Sclessin](/wiki/Sclessin "Sclessin") is the district where the stadium is located. Maurice Dufrasne was the fifth chairman of Standard. He was also the driving force behind the move of Standard from [Grivegnée](/wiki/Grivegn%C3%A9e "Grivegnée") to Sclessin. It was opened in 1909. Initially it was just a field, players had to change in a nearby pub.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

A year later a first stand was built. By 1924 the club could accommodate 24,000 spectators. Around 1970 it reached its maximum capacity with 43,000 spectators.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Sclessin received a new main stand in 1985, and seven years later, in 1992, works started on the stand at the opposite side. Both ends got redeveloped in the late 1990s in preparation of the [Euro 2000 tournament](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Standard_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Derby_de_Wallonie_1.jpg/330px-Derby_de_Wallonie_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Derby_de_Wallonie_1.jpg)

Standard fan group, prior to a 2017 derby match against [Royal Charleroi](/wiki/Royal_Charleroi_S.C. "Royal Charleroi S.C.").

Standard Liège supporters share a fierce rivalry with [RSC Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht"), dubbed the Belgian "[Clasico](/wiki/El_Cl%C3%A1sico "El Clásico")".[[19]](#cite_note-sofoot178773-19) The rivalry not only reflects the traditional geographical one between the two cities of Liège and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), but also a class divide, with Anderlecht being perceived as the team of the bourgeois elite and Standard, based in an industrial district, as the workers club. The two teams were also the two most successful teams in Belgium for long periods until the emergence of [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV").[[19]](#cite_note-sofoot178773-19) Many players have played for both clubs, most notably Standard title winning captain [Steven Defour](/wiki/Steven_Defour "Steven Defour"), who when returning to Sclessin under Anderlecht's purple colours was greeted with a large *[tifo](/wiki/Tifo "Tifo")* with his head decapitated.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Standard also has a traditional city derbies with [RFC Seraing](/wiki/R.F.C._Seraing_(1922) "R.F.C. Seraing (1922)") and [RFC Liège](/wiki/RFC_Li%C3%A8ge "RFC Liège").[[21]](#cite_note-21) In recent years, they have also developed a rivalry with fellow [Walloon](/wiki/Walloons "Walloons") club [Sporting Charleroi](/wiki/R._Charleroi_S.C. "R. Charleroi S.C."), with several matches having been stopped due to crowd disturbances between the two sets of supporters.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Matches with Limburgish clubs [Racing Genk](/wiki/K.R.C._Genk "K.R.C. Genk") and [STVV](/wiki/Sint-Truidense_V.V. "Sint-Truidense V.V.") also are characterised with heightened tensions. This is due to the proximity of Genk and Sint-Truiden with the city of Liège and the historical ties of the mining and steel industries of these regions in Belgium. The rivalry between Standard and Racing Genk was fueled by the events of 17 May 2011.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In this title match Standard winger [Mehdi Carcela](/wiki/Mehdi_Carcela "Mehdi Carcela") was hit in the face with a tackle by Genk defender [Chris Mavinga](/wiki/Chris_Mavinga "Chris Mavinga"). Carcela lost consciousness and was subbed off. Mavinga was not sent off after his reckless intervention. Genk went on to win the title with 1–1 draw, but it left many Standard fans with a sour taste.
